title: Mobility aid quick repair triage
summary: A quick triage for canes, walkers, wheelchairs, crutches, brakes, tips, straps, and seats after evacuation, stairs, rubble, rain, or transport damage.
content:
A quick triage for canes, walkers, wheelchairs, crutches, brakes, tips, straps, and seats after evacuation, stairs, rubble, rain, or transport damage.

This card keeps mobility aid quick repair triage practical: define the owner, protect the vulnerable point, make the result visible, and keep one review time. It is designed for phone use under stress, not for perfect paperwork.

Core sequence: 1) Ask the user what feels unsafe before touching the aid or changing its setup. 2) Check brakes, tips, wheels, loose bolts, torn straps, sharp edges, and wet surfaces in that order. 3) Make only reversible temporary fixes and mark them visibly as temporary. 4) Do a short test on flat ground before using ramps, stairs, crowds, or uneven paths.

Stop point: Do not use mobility aid quick repair triage to justify entering a place that smells unsafe, sounds unstable, is electrically wet, or has been closed by authorities.
materials: small repair pouch for mobility aid reserved for Mobility aid quick repair triage
dated note for the owner of Mobility aid quick repair triage
separate clean storage point for Accessibility · Mobility aid repair
visible stop mark for Mobility aid quick repair triage
steps: Ask the user what feels unsafe before touching the aid or changing its setup.
Check brakes, tips, wheels, loose bolts, torn straps, sharp edges, and wet surfaces in that order.
Make only reversible temporary fixes and mark them visibly as temporary.
Do a short test on flat ground before using ramps, stairs, crowds, or uneven paths.
warnings: Do not treat mobility aid quick repair triage as routine when official instructions, injury, fire, contaminated water, or structural danger change the situation.
Do not keep private names, medicine details, or locations visible longer than mobility aid quick repair triage genuinely requires.
common_mistakes: Beginning mobility aid quick repair triage without a named owner makes good supplies and correct advice hard to use.
Letting old notes guide mobility aid quick repair triage after conditions change can send people toward yesterday’s hazard.
alternatives: If mobility aid quick repair triage cannot be completed, finish only the safest visible marker and write what still needs checking.
If the normal tool is missing, switch to paper, spoken confirmation, and a simpler one-person handover for mobility aid quick repair triage.
failure_signs: People disagree about the current status of mobility aid quick repair triage because the last change was not dated.
The person most affected by mobility aid quick repair triage cannot access the result, read the notice, or ask for help.
when_not_to_use: Do not use mobility aid quick repair triage to justify entering a place that smells unsafe, sounds unstable, is electrically wet, or has been closed by authorities.
Do not continue mobility aid quick repair triage if it prevents a 112 call or hides a serious health warning.
short_term: Complete the first visible result for mobility aid quick repair triage and confirm the next review time before moving to a lower priority task.
long_term: After real use, improve mobility aid quick repair triage by removing steps nobody understood and adding the local contact or storage point that was missing.
if_method_fails: If mobility aid quick repair triage fails, stop the routine, protect the person or area at highest risk, and restart with one owner and one written action.
environment_notes: Adapt mobility aid quick repair triage for apartments, villages, islands, camps, poor lighting, heat, rain, crowds, low battery, and limited mobility.
